coredata.dc:title: Functionalized graphene, quantum dots, and PEDOT:PSS based screen-printed electrode for the endocrine disruptor bisphenol A determination
coredata.dc:creator: Santos, Anderson M.
Feitosa, Maria H.A.
Wong, Ademar
Fatibello-Filho, Orlando
Sotomayor, Maria D.P.T.
Moraes, Fernando C.
coredata.dc:description: Strategies aimed for the monitoring the endocrine disruptor bisphenol A (BPA) become essential due to the high toxicity that this substance can cause in the environment. In this sense, a screen-printed electrode with functionalized graphene, cadmium telluride quantum dots, and polymer (PEDOT:PSS) was manufactured for the determination of the BPA in environmental matrices. The materials that compose the sensor were characterized by fluorescence spectroscopy, X-ray diffraction, FEG-SEM, TEM and FTIR. The electrochemical behavior of the analyte was performed by cyclic voltammetry and the quantification by square-wave voltammetry, respectively. The electroanalytical method showed a linear response in the BPA concentration range from 0.020 to 9.0 µmol L−1 with a limit of detection of 3.4 nmol L−1. Finally, the electroanalytical method was successfully applied to environmental samples for the determination of BPA, with a recovery close to 100%. This demonstrates its good applicability and sensitivity for this type of sample.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene
Quantum dots
Bisphenol A
Electrochemical sensor
Screen-printed electrode

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of functionalized graphene, silver nanoparticles, and quantum dots
Preparation and modification of the SPE sensor
Effect of pH and type of electrolyte
Effect of potential scan rate and determination of kinetic parameters for BPA oxidation
Repeatability, stability, selectivity, and reproducibility studies
Determination of BPA in environmental samples

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Fundação de Amparo à Pesquisa do Estado de São Paulo

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Coordenação de Aperfeiçoamento de Pessoal de Nível Superior

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This study was financed in part by the Coordenação de Aperfeiçoamento de Pessoal de Nível Superior - Brazil ( CAPES ) - Finance Code 001 . The authors gratefully acknowledge the financial support granted by FAPESP (grant numbers 2017/10118–0, 2022/05454–9 , and 2020/01050–5 ), PROPe (Edital nº 13/2022) and INCTDATREN (FAPESP 2014/50945–4 ; CNPq 465571/2014–0 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) Fluorescence spectra for different CdTe@QDs at the excitation wavelength of 340 nm. The CdTe@QDs were obtained in an oven at 150 ºC, varying the time from 60 to180 min. (B) Cyclic voltammograms obtained for BPA 10.0 µmol L−1 solution with different CdTe@QDs in FG-PEDOT:PSS/SPE. Analysis conditions: v = 50 mV s−1 and 0.10 mol L−1 phosphate buffer solution (pH 7.0).
(A) X-ray diffraction for the CdTe@QDs sample. (B) Fluorescence spectra of CdTe@QDs at different excitation wavelengths ranging from 300 nm to 380 nm. Inset: UV-Vis absorbance spectra of CdTe@QDs.
SEM image of (A) carbon ink (SPE), (B) FG, (C-D) AgNPs-FG (E-F) CdTe@QDs-FG and corresponding color map for (G-I) AgNPs-FG and (J-L) CdTe@QDs-FG.
TEM images for (A) CdTe@QDs at 50 nm magnification and (B) CdTe@QDs at 20 nm magnification (C) corresponding histogram of CdTe@QDs diameters.
- EIS diagrams for 1.0 mmol L−1 ferrocene carboxylic acid solution in 0.10 mol L−1 KCl. Frequency range 100 kHz to 0.5 Hz.

SW voltammograms recorded for 1.0 µmol L−1 BPA using 0.10 mol L−1 phosphate buffer solution with pH ranging from 4.0 to 9.0 and CdTe@QDs-FG-PEDOT:PSS/SPE sensor. Parameters: a = 50 mV, f = 15 Hz and ΔE s = 5 mV s−1. Inserted: E p vs. pH and I p vs. pH.

Cyclic voltammograms at different potential scan rates ranging from 10 to 100 mV s−1 using the CdTe@QDs-FG-PEDOT:PSS/SPE sensor for 10.0 μmol L−1 BPA in 0.10 mol L−1 phosphate buffer solution (pH 7.0). Insert: graphic of log I p vs. log v.

(A) SW voltammograms obtained using the CdTe@QDs-FG-PEDOT:PSS/SPE sensor in 0.10 mol L−1 phosphate buffer solution (pH 7.0) containing different concentrations for BPA: (0−15) 0.0, 0.020, 0.050, 0.070, 0.10, 0.20, 0.40, 0.60, 0.80, 1.0, 2.0, 3.0, 5.0, 7.0 and 9.0 µmol L−1, and (B) Analytical curve. SWV: a = 40 mV, f = 20 Hz, ΔE s = 5 mV and t
acc = 30 s.
Proposed electrode reaction for the oxidation of BPA.
Comparison between electroanalytical methods for determination of BPA.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: DPV: differential pulse voltammetry; GCE: glassy carbon electrode; ABPE: acetylene black paste electrode; MWCNT: multi-walled carbon nanotube; Poly(CTA): poly(chromotropic acid); MIP: molecularly imprinted; N-GS: nitrogen-doped graphene sheets; GR: graphene.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Determination of BPA in river water samples using SWV and the CdTe@QDs-FG-PEDOT:PSS/SPE sensor.

head.ce:title: Functionalized graphene, quantum dots, and PEDOT:PSS based screen-printed electrode for the endocrine disruptor bisphenol A determination

ce:author.ce:contributor-role: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation, Writing − original draft, Writing − review & editing
Supervision, Writing − review & editing, Funding acquisition

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Federal University of São Carlos (UFSCar), Rod. Washington Luís, km 235, P. O. Box 676, 13560-970 São Carlos, SP, Brazil

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemistry, Federal University of São Carlos (UFSCar)
sa:affiliation.sa:address-line: Rod. Washington Luís, km 235, P. O. Box 676

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Federal University of São Carlos (UFSCar), Rod. Washington Luís, km 235, P. O. Box 676, 13560–970, São Carlos, SP-Brazil

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Chemistry, State University of São Paulo (UNESP), Rua Prof. Francisco Degni, 55, 14801-970 Araraquara, SP, Brazil

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Chemistry, State University of São Paulo (UNESP)

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute of Chemistry, State University of São Paulo (UNESP), Rua Prof. Francisco Degni, 55, 14801–970, Araraquara, SP-Brazil

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Strategies aimed for the monitoring the endocrine disruptor bisphenol A (BPA) become essential due to the high toxicity that this substance can cause in the environment. In this sense, a screen-printed electrode with functionalized graphene, cadmium telluride quantum dots, and polymer (PEDOT:PSS) was manufactured for the determination of the BPA in environmental matrices. The materials that compose the sensor were characterized by fluorescence spectroscopy, X-ray diffraction, FEG-SEM, TEM and FTIR. The electrochemical behavior of the analyte was performed by cyclic voltammetry and the quantification by square-wave voltammetry, respectively. The electroanalytical method showed a linear response in the BPA concentration range from 0.020 to 9.0 µmol L−1 with a limit of detection of 3.4 nmol L−1. Finally, the electroanalytical method was successfully applied to environmental samples for the determination of BPA, with a recovery close to 100%. This demonstrates its good applicability and sensitivity for this type of sample.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Bisphenol A (BPA), an endocrine disruptor, is a very important synthetic organic compound in the industry due to its high demand, being widely used in the production of polycarbonate plastics and epoxy resins [1]. Furthermore, its applicability covers production processes not only in the food industry (i.e. food and beverage packaging, bottles, and cutlery), but also in the automotive, electronics, and toy industries [2]. The great concern with this compound is when it comes into direct contact with humans and other living beings in the environment, mainly through bottles, pots, and plastic bags used in the storage of food and beverages. Thus, with the inadequate disposal of these materials in the environment, the presence of BPA has increased in rivers, lakes, seas, and the soil of different regions of the world [3,4].

In this context, the development of methods to detect and monitor emerging contaminants in environmental matrices, such as BPA, is of utmost importance. Conventional analytical methods such as chromatography, are relatively expensive for this type of analysis, which normally employs highly toxic organic solvents [5]. Thus, the development of alternative methods such as electrochemical using dedicated sensors has proven to be an excellent alternative for the detection and quantification of electroactive organic compounds, in addition to the fact that in most cases it does not require a more elaborated pre-treatment of the sample as in other analytical techniques [6]. Electroanalytical methods are based on several techniques capable of providing the determination and/or detection of an analyte of environmental interest with high precision, accuracy, reliability, rapid confirmation of results, and traceability of analytical data [7]. The rapid growth of research related to electrochemical sensing is due to a fundamental characteristic in relation to nanotechnology [8–10], because with the nanoscale structuring it became possible to improve morphologies and sizes of conductor/semiconductor materials that are fundamental to amplify the signal through conductive properties, catalytic and chemical interactions between surfaces [9–11].

Considering that the use of electroanalytical methods has become increasingly used as environmental monitoring strategies, an emerging technology appears in this line of research that aims to manufacture disposable electrodes from printed materials based on screen printing technology, aimed mainly at the portability and miniaturization of electrochemical sensors, obtaining results with excellent response and low cost [12,13]. This technology is already well established in scientific academies and explored in the commercial field, such as the glucose biosensor used by people with diabetes [14]. In this context, other devices, and sensors printed with carbonaceous materials offer rapid analysis of samples and are versatile in the face of analysis demand and the complexity and compatibility with real analysis matrices.

The printed sensor is composed of carbon ink and other modifiers to obtain detection at low concentration values as often found in real matrices. In this context, with a focus on enhancing sensor response, various materials have been utilized as modifiers. These include carbonaceous materials (graphene, carbon black, carbon, and nanotubes), metallic nanoparticles (Au, Ag, Pt, and Pd) [15–18], conductive polymers (poly(3,4-ethylenedioxythiophene), and polystyrene sulfonate (PEDOT:PSS)) [19], and semiconductor nanoparticles (quantum dots of CdSe, and CdTe) [20,21]. These modifications tend to promote a synergistic effect between the materials, causing an improvement in conductivity and/or an increase in the electroactive area. This behavior can lead to an increase in peak current magnitude, resulting in the achievement of low detection limit values.

Functionalized graphene (FG) is already well established in the literature as one of the best carbonaceous materials that can be applied in the manufacture of electrochemical sensors, as it has excellent physicochemical properties, such as high mechanical strength, excellent thermal and electrical conductivities, large surface area, and significant electric field effect. Furthermore, when combined with metallic nanoparticles or quantum dots, it promotes a pronounced synergy effect on the detection response.

CdTe quantum dots (CdTe@QDs) are fluorescent semiconductor nanoparticles that have a broad emission spectrum [22] and nanoscale dimensions in the 2–6 nm range, and have good stability and water solubility (ideal for forming dispersions) [23]. They are platforms that have high quantum yields providing greater sensitivity and chemical stability [24]. When combined with graphene, it promotes greater efficiency in the surface area, making it more catalytic through the increase of catalytic sites, which can be characterized as an ideal material to give greater functionality to the sensor [25].

In this work, a new electrochemical device based on a screen-printed electrode (SPE) modified with functionalized graphene (FG) and cadmium telluride quantum dots (CdTe@QDs) is proposed for the determination of BPA in river water samples with high sensitivity, accuracy, repeatability, and low cost.

All solutions were prepared with deionized water (resistivity > 18 MΩ cm at 25 °C) obtained from a Milli-Q Direct-0.3 purification system (Millipore, Burlington, VT, USA). BPA, PEDOT:PSS, tellurium powder (Te), cadmium chloride (CdCl2), sodium borohydride (NaBH4), silver nitrate (AgNO3), and C3H6O2S (3-mercaptopropanoic acid) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (St. Louis, MO, USA). Salts, bases, and alcohols such as monopotassium phosphate (KH2PO4), potassium phosphate dibasic (K2HPO4), sodium hydroxide (NaOH), and isopropanol (i-PrOH) were acquired from Synth (Diadema, SP, Brazil). Graphene, carbon ink, and silver/silver chloride (Ag/AgCl) ink were purchased from Graphene Supermarket (Ronkonkoma, NY, USA), Electrodag 423SS from Henkel Ltd. (Irvine, CA, USA), and Gwent Electronic Materials Ltd. (Parsippany, NJ, USA), respectively.

Fluorescence analyses of CdTe@QDs suspensions were performed by a spectrofluorimeter (Shimadzu RF-5301PC, Kyoto, Japan) while absorbance analyses were performed using a spectrophotometer (Shimadzu UV-2550, Kyoto, Japan). The identification of functional groups (wavenumber range of 4000–500 cm–1) was obtained using a Fourier transform infrared spectrophotometer (FTIR) - IRPrestige-21 (Shimadzu Instrument, Kyoto – Japan). Scanning electron microscopy (SEM, Philip XL-30 FEG) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM, FEI Tecnai G2 F20, Eindhoven, The Netherlands) techniques were used in the morphological characterization. Electrochemical experiments were performed on an Autolab Metrohm potentiostat/galvanostat (PGSTAT 128 N, Eco Chemie, Utrecht, The Netherlands) controlled by NOVA 2.1 software.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of functionalized graphene, silver nanoparticles, and quantum dots
ce:section.ce:para: The functionalization of graphene (FG) was performed as described by Santos et al. [26]. First, a mass of graphene equivalent to 100 mg was measured and transferred to a beaker containing a mixture of 50 mL of concentrated nitric acid and 50 mL of concentrated sulfuric acid. This solution remained under magnetic stirring for approximately 12 h at room temperature (25 ºC). Finally, the FG suspension was vacuum filtered and washed with deionized water by centrifugation until the final suspension reached a pH close to 7.0 followed by a drying step at 100 ºC.

Silver nanoparticles (AgNPs) were synthesized as described in the literature [27]. For this, solutions of 0.20 mmol L–1 AgNO3 and 5.0 mmol L–1 NaBH4 were prepared. Thus, individually 48 mL of a 0.20 mmol L–1 of AgNO3 solution and 2.0 mL of a 5.0 mmol L–1 NaBH4 solution were stored in two flasks, bubbled with nitrogen gas, and left in an ice bath for approximately 30 min. Subsequently, under stirring, the NaBH4 solution was slowly added to the AgNO3 solution, forming a yellowish colloidal suspension, indicating the formation of the silver nanoparticles (AgNPs). Finally, the suspension was stirred for another 30 min and stored in a dark bottle.

CdTe@QDs were synthesized as described by Santos et al. [21], employing the reaction of CdCl2 with NaHTe in the presence of C3H6O2S. Thus, initially, 10 mL of a 0.10 mol L−1 of NaBH4 solution was prepared and nitrogen gas was bubbled through the solution. In sequence, 0.40 mmol of Te powder was added to the NaBH4 solution. This solution was stirred for approximately 2 h until the tellurium powder (black) disappeared and the resulting NaHTe in the light purple supernatant was generated. In parallel, 40 mL of a 5.0 mmol L−1 CdCl2 solution was prepared and 30 µL of C3H6O2S was added and the pH of the solution was adjusted to 9.0 using a 1.0 mol L−1 NaOH solution. This solution was also purged with nitrogen gas. Finally, 2.0 mL of NaHTe solution was slowly dropped into the CdCl2 solution. The mixture was placed in a Teflon-coated stainless-steel autoclave and heated at a temperature of 150 °C from 60 to 180 min. Subsequently, CdTe nanocrystals were isolated by precipitation with i-PrOH, followed by centrifugation (6000 rpm for 10 min). The precipitate was dried and redispersed in the same volume of water as in the initial synthesis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and modification of the SPE sensor
ce:section.ce:para: The screen-printed electrode (SPE) manufactured was prepared as previously described by Wong et al. [28] containing the counter electrode, working electrode (Ø = 3.0 mm), and pseudo-reference electrode, all integrated into a single platform. A cutting printer (Silhouette CAMEO 3), polyester sheet, vinyl adhesive sheet, carbon conductive inks, and Ag/AgCl conductive inks were used to fabricate the SPE. The modification of the SPE in turn was performed by the addition of 8.0 µL of the different dispersions, such as: FG-PEDOT:PSS, AgNPs-FG-PEDOT:PSS, or CdTe@QDs-FG-PEDOT:PSS on the surface of the working electrode.

For the preparation of the different dispersions, the amount of each material was optimized: FG (0.50–2.0 mg), AgNPs suspension (200–800 µL), and CdTe@QDs (200–800 µL). For this, in all cases the final volume of the dispersion was completed to 1.0 mL with 20 µL of PEDOT:PSS and ultrapure water. This volume of 20 µL of PEDOT:PSS was selected because it showed good stability of the film without signal loss.

Thus, after the whole process of optimization the dispersion containing FG-PEDOT:PSS was prepared from 1.0 mg of FG, 20 µL of PEDOT:PSS, and 980 µL of ultrapure water. The second containing AgNPs-FG-PEDOT:PSS was prepared from 1.0 mg of FG, 380 µL of ultrapure water, 20 µL PEDOT:PSS, and 600 µL of a solution containing AgNPs. The dispersion containing CdTe@QDs-FG-PEDOT:PSS was prepared from 1.0 mg FG, 180 µL of ultrapure water, 20 µL PEDOT:PSS, and 800 µL CdTe@QDs.

The river water samples from São Carlos, Brazil (the Monjolinho-River 1, 21°59′11.0′’S 47°52′52.1′’W) and Araraquara, Brazil (the Chibarro-River 2, 21°48′25.0′’S 48°10′21.7′’W) were collected with the help of a flask coupled to a rod and, transferred to amber polyethylene vials and stored in a refrigerator at a temperature of 3–4 °C.

Posteriorly, each collected river water was divided into 10 mL flasks and different aliquots of 1.0 mmol L–1 BPA standard solutions were carefully added to each flask. Finally, 250 µL of the sample was added to the electrochemical cell and completed with the supporting electrolyte (phosphate buffer solution at pH 7.0), and the electrochemical measurement was performed using the CdTe@QDs-FG-PEDOT:PSS/SPE sensor.

Initially, the optical characteristics of the synthesized CdTe quantum dots (CdTe@QDs) (varying only in the oven time) were evaluated by fluorescence spectroscopy ( Fig. 1A). As seen in Fig. 1A, the fluorescence spectra for the different CdTe@QDs showed emission peak varying the wavelength (λ) from 521 to 612 nm, i.e., going from green to red, respectively. Subsequently, the different CdTe@QDs were used together with FG in the modification of the SPE aiming at an improvement in the analytical signal (increase in the peak current magnitude) for the determination of the BPA. As seen in Fig. 1B, using different CdTe@QDs, the cyclic voltammograms for 1.0 mmol L–1 BPA oxidation presented almost the same peak current magnitude. Therefore, the orange CdTe@QDs (582 nm) were selected for further studies.

The synthesized materials (CdTe@QDs) (λ = 582) were characterized by X-ray diffraction (XRD), UV-Vis absorption, and photoluminescence (PL). X-ray diffraction analyses are shown in Fig. 2A. As seen, there are three very evident peaks located at 24º, 40º, and 47º. These peaks are attributed to the (111), (220), and (311) crystalline cubic phase planes of the CdTe@QDs (PDF 65–1046) [29]. The optical characteristics of CdTe@QDs (λ = 582), such as absorption and fluorescence, which were evaluated using UV-Vis measurements and fluorescence spectra, are shown in Fig. 2B. It also shows the fluorescence spectra of CdTe@QDs excited at different wavelengths (λ): 300 nm, 320 nm, 340 nm, 360 nm, and 380 nm, which, in all cases, the fluorescence emission response occurred at the wavelength of 582 nm. The maximum wavelength was obtained with an excitation wavelength of 340 nm.

In Fig. 2B (insert), the UV-Vis absorption spectra of CdTe@QDs nanocrystals are shown. In this case, a well-defined absorption maximum was observed at λ = 538 nm. The amount of energy absorbed in the quantum confinement is related to the size of the nanocrystals, which, as they increase in size, move to higher λ values. Thus, to evaluate the size of the CdTe@QDs nanocrystals, a theoretical method based on the maximum absorption of UV-Vis was used, as reported by Yu et al. [30], which indicates an estimated value of the QDs size. The relationship between the particle diameter (D) and the UV-Vis wavelength (λ) can be visualized in the following equation (Eq. (1)):
(1) D = (9·8127 × 10−7)λ3 – (1·7147 × 10−3)λ2 + (1·0064)λ – 194·84
Thus, using equation (Eq. (1)) the average diameter (D) obtained for aqueous suspension of CdTe@QDs (λ = 538 nm) was 3.1 nm.

Morphological characterization of carbon ink and nanomaterials, FG, AgNPs-FG, CdTe@QDs-FG, and CdTe@QDs were performed by FEG-SEM and/or TEM. Fig. 3 shows the FEG-SEM images of (A) carbon ink, (B) FG, (C-D) AgNPs-FG, and (E-F) CdTe@QDs-FG. The carbon ink (Fig. 3A) showed a homogeneous and dense morphology. In Fig. 3B, the FG wrinkled sheets were arranged in different sizes. In Fig. 3C-D, several AgNPs grains (small dots) dispersed in the FG leaves are observed. Whereas, Fig. 3E-F shows a cluster of small nanoparticles, referring to the CdTe@QDs on the FG sheets. In the sequence, the color map images of the different materials AgNPs-FG (Fig. 3G-I) and CdTe@QDs-FG (Fig. 3J-L) are shown.

As seen in Fig. 4A and B the TEM images of the CdTe@QDs, the quantum dots are scattered with a small spherical particle-sized distribution. Particle diameters were measured directly from TEM images using ImageJ software (Fig. 4C). In this way, it was possible to obtain an average value of the CdTe nanocrystals of 3.0 nm, which is in close agreement with the calculated theoretical value of 3.1.

Finally, FTIR spectra for the different modifications were recorded in the range of 4000–500 cm−1, as shown in Fig. S1. The spectra for the infrared absorption bands at 3723 cm−1, 3305 cm−1, and 1420 cm−1 are related to the presence of the O–H group [31,32]. The absorption band at 2940 cm−1, 1378 cm−1, and 870 cm−1 is caused by the stretching vibration of C–H [31,32]. The bands corresponding to CO stretching vibration, aromatic CC bending, C–O stretching, and C–O–C stretching were identified at 1730 cm−1, 1633 cm−1, 1226 cm−1, and 1048 cm−1, respectively [31–33]. Finally, the bands at 1553 cm−1, and 664 cm−1 represent the stretching vibration of the compound NO and C–S, respectively [31–33]. As can be seen, the C–S absorption band was intensified with the addition of PEDOT:PSS and CdTe@QDs, due to its presence in the compound or in the synthesis, as is the case with CdTe@QDs that uses 3-mercaptopropanoic acid.

The working electrodes (SPE, FG-PEDOT:PSS/SPE, AgNPs-FG-PEDOT:PSS/SPE, and CdTe@QDs-FG-PEDOT:PSS/SPE) were conditioned to experiments that aimed to estimate the area of electroactive surface together with the Randles-Sevcik equation [34] (Eq. (2)), in which the cyclic voltammetry technique was used for this study at a potential scan rate between 10 and 200 mV s–1 for a 1.0 mmol L–1 of ferrocene carboxylic acid solution as electrochemical probe and aqueous 0.10 mmol L–1 KCl as supporting electrolyte (see Fig. S2).
(2) ΔI p = ± (2·69 × 105) n 3/2 A D 1/2 C v 1/2

where ΔI p is anodic or cathodic peak current, n is the number of electrons transferred, A is the electroactive area (cm2), D is the diffusion coefficient of ferrocene carboxylic acid in the 0.1 mol L–1 KCl solution (4.5 × 10−6 cm2 s−1), C is the ferrocene carboxylic acid concentration (mol cm−3) and v is the potential scan rate (V s−1).

The average of the slopes (I pa and I pc) of the curves ΔI p vs. v 1/2 for the ferrocene carboxylic acid oxidation process using SPE, FG-PEDOT:PSS/SPE, AgNPs-FG-PEDOT:PSS/SPE and CdTe@QDs-FG-PEDOT:PSS/SPE sensors were 5.2 × 10−5 A V−1/2 s1/2; 8.3 × 10−5 A V−1/2 s1/2; 9.7 × 10−5 A V−1/2 s1/2 and 1.0 × 10−4 A V−1/2 s1/2, respectively (see Fig. S2). The electroactive areas for the electrodes were calculated from those obtained values, which were 0.091 cm2 (SPE), 0.14 cm2 (FG-PEDOT:PSS/SPE), 0.17 cm2 (AgNPs-FG-PEDOT:PSS/SPE), and 0.18 cm2 (CdTe@QDs-FG-PEDOT:PSS/SPE), respectively. Therefore, the data obtained suggest that the modification of the SPE with FG, AgNPs, and/or CdTe@QDs significantly increased the electrochemical response for the ferrocene carboxylic acid redox probe, indicating that the increase in the peak current represents an increase in the electroactive area when compared to the unmodified SPE, justifying its use for the BPA oxidation as a strategy to improve electrochemical detection.

To further explore the electrochemical behavior of the different modifications, electrochemical experiments were carried out using electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS). These analyzes were carried out in the frequency range from 100 KHz to 0.5 Hz and amplitude of 10 mV using a 1.0 mmol L−1 of ferrocene carboxylic acid solution as a redox probe in 0.10 mol L−1 KCl, in which the applied potential was selected through CV (half-wave potential, E p/2). This technique has been applied as a way of characterizing the heterogeneous electron transfer at the electrode/solution interface [35]. In Fig. 5, the Nyquist diagrams for the different electrodes are represented, and as can be seen, these diagrams present two very distinct parts, a linear part at lower frequencies referring to the diffusional control of the electroactive species, and a semicircle at high frequencies, which is attributed to the parallel combination between the charge transfer resistance (R ct) and the capacitance of the electrical double layer. In this way, the semicircle corresponds to the limited transfer of electrons, and its diameter is equal to R ct, that is, the larger the diameter of the semicircle, the greater the R ct [35].

The impedance spectra were fitted employing a Randles equivalent circuit (adapted), see Fig. 5. (inset). This circuit includes the solution resistance (R s) in series with the parallel combination of R ct and a Warburg impedance (W) with a constant phase element (CPE). The obtained R ct were 1290, 824, 483, and 326 Ω, for the SPE; FG-PEDOT:PSS/SPE, AgNPs-FG-PEDOT:PSS/SPE and CdTe@QDs-FG-PEDOT:PSS/SPE, respectively. As can be seen, with the modification of the SPE there was a decrease in the semicircle, that is, a decrease in R ct, thus indicating an increase in electrode conductivity.

The EIS was also used to calculate the apparent heterogeneous electron transfer rate constant (k 0) for each sensor according to equation (Eq. (3)):
(3)
k
0
=
RT
F
2
R
ct
AC
where k
0 is the heterogeneous electron transfer constant (cm s−1), A is the electrode geometric area (0.071 cm2), C is the concentration of ferrocene carboxylic acid solution (1.0 µmol cm−3), F is the Faraday constant (96,485 C mol−1), R is the gas constant (8.314 J mol−1 K−1), T is the Thermodynamic temperature (298.15 K), and R ct is the values of charge transfer resistance already cited.

The k 0 is a kinetic measure of the time required for the redox couple to reach equilibrium. Thus, time and k 0 are inversely proportional, therefore the higher the value of k 0, the shorter the time it takes for the redox couple to reach the equilibrium. The calculated k 0 values for the electrodes were 2.9 × 10−3 cm s−1 (SPE), 4.5 × 10−3 cm s−1 (FG-PEDOT:PSS/SPE), 7.8 × 10− 3 cm s−1 (AgNPs-FG-PEDOT:PSS/SPE) and 1.1 × 10−2 cm s−1 (CdTe@QDs-FG-PEDOT:PSS/SPE). The most significant k 0 value is related to CdTe@QDs-FG-PEDOT:PSS/SPE, indicating a faster electron transfer.

For this study, cyclic voltammetry (CV) and square-wave voltammetry (SWV) techniques were used to evaluate the electrochemical behavior of BPA with different electrodes (SPE, FG-PEDOT:PSS/SPE, AgNPs-FG-PEDOT:PSS/SPE and CdTe@QDs-FG-PEDOT:PSS/SPE) in 0.10 mol L−1 phosphate buffer solution (pH 7.0).

As seen in Fig. 6A, cyclic voltammograms obtained in a potential range from 0 to + 0.45 V and a potential scan rate of 50 mV s−1 showed well-defined oxidation peaks at potentials between 0.30 and 0.32 V. In addition, the BPA did not show reduction peaks during the cathodic potential scan, demonstrating an irreversible oxidation reaction. Furthermore, AgNPs-FG-PEDOT:PSS/SPE and CdTe@QDs-FG-PEDOT:PSS/SPE showed a higher magnitude of the oxidation current peak (I pox), with an increase of 3.4 and 3.8 times when compared to SPE, respectively. These results indicate the efficiency in a signal gain of the analyte using CdTe@QDs-FG-PEDOT:PSS and AgNPs-FG-PEDOT:PSS for the determination of BPA.

Subsequently, to evaluate the best modification for the SPE, the determination of BPA was carried out using the SWV after successive injections of standard solutions (0.20–1.0 µmol L−1) with the different electrodes (Fig. S3). Thus, the dependence between the analytical signal and concentration resulted in the analytical curve shown in Fig. 6B, where the analytical signal (peak current magnitude) recorded for the BPA increased linearly with increasing concentration in the electrochemical cell for all electrodes. In addition, it is also possible to observe that CdTe@QDs-FG-PEDOT:PSS/SPE showed greater sensitivity with an analytical curve slope of 1.1. Based on these results, the CdTe@QDs-FG-PEDOT:PSS/SPE was selected as the best candidate to work with due to its higher sensitivity and, consequently, the potential for achieving lower detection limits.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of pH and type of electrolyte
ce:section.ce:para: Initially, the influence of pH for 1.0 µmol L−1 BPA oxidation was investigated by SWV, in which the pH of the supporting electrolyte ranged from 4.0 to 9.0 ( Fig. 7). The optimal pH found was pH 7.0, and for this reason, it was selected for the rest of the analyses. The graph inserted in Fig. 7 represents the E p vs. pH, in which, it can be observed that the peak potential variation (E p) as a function of pH changed linearly with a slope of − 0.052 V pH–1. This slope was close to the Nernstian theoretical value of −0.059 V pH–1 typical for redox reactions involving the same number of electrons and protons, thus suggesting that the same number of protons and electrons are involved in the electro-oxidation of BPA. Thus, the probable mechanism for the electro-oxidation reaction of BPA is represented in Scheme 1 involving two electrons and two protons, as described in the literature [36]. After the pH study, a BPA detection test was performed in Britton-Robinson buffer at pH 7.0 to assess whether the solution composition influenced the analyte detection (Fig. S4). Thus, the phosphate-buffered solution provided the highest peak current magnitude in the SWV experiments. Therefore, this buffer solution was selected for the rest of the studies.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of potential scan rate and determination of kinetic parameters for BPA oxidation
ce:section.ce:para: The effect of the potential scan rate on the electrochemical behavior of the CdTe@QDs-FG-PEDOT:PSS/SPE sensor was investigated by the cyclic voltammetry, varying the potential scan rate from 10 to 100 mV s−1 for a 10.0 μmol L−1 BPA in 0.10 mol L−1 phosphate buffer solution (pH 7.0). As seen in Fig. 8, with an increasing potential scan rate, there was a linear increase in peak BPA current. Furthermore, when plotting the logarithm of the anodic peak current versus the logarithm of the scan rate (log ΔI p vs. log v), a slope of 0.59 was obtained, as seen in Fig. 8B. Value is close to the theoretical value of 0.50, characteristic for a diffusion-controlled process.

Next, the study of the potential scan rate (Fig. 8) was employed to determine the kinetic parameters involved in the electro-oxidation of BPA using CdTe@QDs-FG-PEDOT:PSS/SPE, such as charge transfer coefficient (α), and heterogeneous electron transfer constant (k
s ). To achieve this, Laviron theory for irreversible systems was used, where the peak potential is linearly dependent on the logarithm of the potential scan rate, E
pa
vs. log v (Eq. (4)) [37]:
(4)
E
p
=
E
º ’
−
RT
α nF
ln
RT
k
s
α nF
+
RT
α nF
ln
v
where n is the number of electrons involved in the redox process, Eº’ is the formal standard potential, v is the potential scan rate (V s−1), and R, T, and F have already been mentioned previously.

As can be seen from the potential scan rate study (Fig. 8A), the peak potential (E
p ) changed positively with the increasing of potential scan rate. Thus, obtaining a good linear relationship between E
p and the natural logarithm of the potential scan rate (ln v) where the regression equation was E
p = 0.399 + 0.0229 ln v (V s−1) with a correlation coefficient of 0.986 (Fig. 8C). Thus, comparing the slope obtained from the E p vs. ln v curves with the slope of the Laviron equation (Eq. (4)), a value of 1.13 for αn was obtained. Assuming a value of α equal to 0.5, a value typically determined for the oxidation of organic molecules, the number of electrons involved in the oxidation of BPA was calculated as being equal to 2.2 (≈ 2.0), which is consistent with the previously proposed reaction (Scheme 1). In sequence, knowing the value of n, the value of α was calculated as 0.56.

Then, knowing the value of αn, k
s was calculated. This calculation was carried out by comparing the linear coefficient obtained from the relationship between E
p
versus ln v, with the linear coefficient given by the Laviron equation (Eq. (4)). Where, the formal standard potential value (Eº’ = 0.297 V), was obtained from the intercept of the E
p
versus v curve. Thus, the ks value obtained was 0.52 s−1. This result proves the efficiency in promoting the transfer of electrons between BPA and the electrode surface.

Finally, the surface coverage of the CdTe@QDs-FG-PEDOT:PSS/SPE film (Г) can be calculated from the charge estimated by integrating the BPA oxidation peak by cyclic voltammetry (E
p = 0.14–0.41; v = 50 mV s−1) and applied in Eq. (5) [38]. The Γ value obtained was 9.40 × 10−10 mol cm2, demonstrating that the sensor has good film coverage on the electrode surface, which contributes to physical and chemical stability during electrochemical measurements.
(5)
Г =
Q
nFA
where Q is the charge and A is the geometric area of the electrode surface, n and F has already been defined previously.

The parameters that affected the SWV response, such as amplitude (a), frequency (f), and potential increment (ΔE s) were optimized. The optimum values obtained for these parameters using 1.0 µmol L−1 BPA in 0.1 mol L−1 phosphate buffer (pH = 7.0) solution with a CdTe@QDs-FG-PEDOT:PSS/SPE sensor, were: a = 40 mV, f = 20 Hz and ΔE s = 5 mV (see Table S1).

To obtain a voltammetric procedure with greater sensitivity, detectability, and stability for the determination of BPA, the possibility of using a pre-concentration step of the analyte on the electrode surface was evaluated. Thus, the application of pre-concentration potentials ranging from − 0.2–0.4 V and open circuit potential (OCP) were compared, all with fixed stirring/accumulation time (t
acc ) (30 s). From the graph in Fig. S5A, it is possible to observe that there was a variation in the magnitude of the peak current against the different pre-concentration potentials (−0.2–0.4 V), but there was no significant increase when compared to the use of OCP conditions. Consequently, it was decided to use only the stirring step. Subsequently, the t
acc ranging from 10 to 60 s was evaluated. As seen in Fig. S5B, with a t
acc of 30 s, it was possible to obtain an increase in the magnitude of the peak current followed by an improvement in the stabilization of the current. Based on these observations, it was decided to work with the stirring/accumulation process for 30 s before each measurement.

After the optimization of all experimental conditions, the determination of BPA was conducted by SWV after successive injections of standard solutions using the CdTe@QDs-FG-PEDOT:PSS/SPE sensor in 0.10 mol L−1 phosphate buffer solution (pH 7.0). As seen in Fig. 9A, the analytical signal (peak current magnitude) recorded for BPA increased progressively with the BPA concentration in the electrochemical cell. Thus, the dependence between the peak current magnitude (analytical signal) and the analyte concentration (BPA) resulted in an analytical curve (n = 3) shown in Fig. 9B. In this way, two linear ranges (r = 994 and r = 0.998) were obtained: one from 0.020 to 1.0 µmol L−1 (range 1) and another from 1.0 to 9.0 µmol L−1 (range 2). The limit of detection (LOD) of 3.4 nmol L−1 was calculated based on the IUPAC [39] recommendations using the relationship 3 × (σ/m), where m is the sensitivity of the analytical curve and σ is the standard deviation of 10 measurements performed with the supporting electrolyte (blank solution). The analytical equations associated with the calibration graph were:
(6)
I p (µA) = 0·012 + 1·0[BPA] (µmol L−1) r = 0·994 (range 1)
(7)
I p (µA) = −0·56 + 0·47[BPA] (µmol L−1) r = 0·998 (range 2)

The analytical performance of the proposed voltammetric method using the CdTe@QDs-FG-PEDOT:PSS/SPE with other methods reported in the literature for the determination of BPA is presented in Table 1 [40–44]. As seen above, the developed CdTe@QDs-FG-PEDOT:PSS/SPE sensor presented the best set of results with some exceptions in terms of sensitivity or linear concentration range. Among the main advantages of SPE modified with CdTe@QDs-FG-PEDOT:PSS are high stability and selectivity, easy preparation (electrode modification), excellent repeatability, and reproducibility as shown below.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Repeatability, stability, selectivity, and reproducibility studies
ce:section.ce:para: The repeatability, stability, selectivity and reproducibility studies of the CdTe@QDs-FG-PEDOT:PSS/SPE sensor was evaluated by the SWV method using a 1.0 µmol L−1 BPA in 0.10 mol L−1 phosphate buffer solution (pH 7.0). To estimate the repeatability, a sequence of 20 electrochemical measurements (n = 20) were performed. In this case, the study presented values of relative standard deviations (RSDs) equal to 3.5% (Fig. S6A). Thus, it is possible to notice the excellent precision of the measurements using the proposed method for the determination of the BPA. Next, to evaluate the stability of the sensor, four electrodes were prepared, modified, and stored in a desiccator for 0, 5, 10, and 15 days at room temperature (25 °C). After this period, a decrease in the peak current magnitude of 3.3% was observed (Fig. S6B), which demonstrates the high stability of the developed sensor.

The selectivity of the proposed sensor was evaluated in the presence of different potential interferents (humic acid, metals, cations, and anions) in a concentration ratio of 1:10 (analyte: potential interference). In this case, the error values obtained ranged from − 1.7% to + 1.3% in all cases (see Table S2), indicating that the CdTe@QDs-FG-PEDOT:PSS/SPE sensor can be efficiently applied for the determination of the BPA in the presence of these potential interferents. Finally, the reproducibility of the preparation of the sensor (CdTe@QDs-FG-PEDOT:PSS/SPE) was evaluated by comparing the electrochemical response (peak current magnitude) obtained from the modification (CdTe@QDs-FG-PEDOT:PSS) of five different electrodes (SPE) obtained with the same manufacturing procedures. The sensors showed similar peak current magnitudes with an RSD of 2.2% (Fig. S7), thus demonstrating the excellent reproducibility of preparation and modification of the electrochemical sensors.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of BPA in environmental samples
ce:section.ce:para: To verify the effectiveness of the proposed voltammetric method (SWV), the CdTe@QDs-FG-PEDOT:PSS/SPE sensor was applied in the determination of the BPA in environmental samples (river water). For this, samples from two different rivers (River 1: Monjolinho River and River 2: Chibarro River) were used and fortified with three known concentration levels of BPA (0.10, 1.0, and 6.0 μmol L−1) and analyzed. In this case, the recovery percentages were between 97% and 110% (n = 3) ( Table 2), indicating that the developed method can quantify BPA in river water samples without possible effects of interferences present in this type of matrices.

Finally, as seen in Table 2, the samples were analyzed by the comparative method (HPLC-UV), and the error between these two methods ranged from − 9.0–10%. Furthermore, the results obtained for the two methods were statistically compared by applying the paired Student's t-test at a confidence level of 95%. Where, the experimental value obtained was 0.24, less than the critical value of 2.6. Therefore, it can be concluded that there is no significant difference between the results obtained using the two analytical methods. Thus, the potential of the method developed using the CdTe@QDs-FG-PEDOT:PSS/SPE sensor for the determination of BPA in river water samples is clear.

Based on the results obtained in this work, it is possible to observe that the SPE modified with CdTe@QDs-FG-PEDOT:PSS significantly improved the electrochemical signal of the BPA, allowing its determination with high detectability, selectivity, precision, and a wide concentration range. Therefore, the developed methodology presents itself as a reliable and advantageous alternative for the detectability of BPA in environmental samples with a recovery of nearly 100%.

Anderson M. Santos: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation, Writing − original draft, Writing − review & editing. Maria H.A. Feitosa: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing − review & editing. Ademar Wong: Validation, Writing − review & editing. Orlando Fatibello-Filho: Writing − review & editing. Maria D. P. T. Sotomayor: Writing − review & editing. Fernando C. Moraes: Supervision, Writing − review & editing, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This study was financed in part by the Coordenação de Aperfeiçoamento de Pessoal de Nível Superior - Brazil (CAPES) - Finance Code 001. The authors gratefully acknowledge the financial support granted by FAPESP (grant numbers 2017/10118–0, 2022/05454–9, and 2020/01050–5), PROPe (Edital nº 13/2022) and INCTDATREN (FAPESP 2014/50945–4; CNPq 465571/2014–0).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.snb.2023.134745.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
Supplementary material
.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: H.-s Yin, Y.-l Zhou, S.-y Ai, Preparation and characteristic of cobalt phthalocyanine modified carbon paste electrode for bisphenol A detection, Journal of Electroanalytical Chemistry, 626 (2009) 80–88.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: General expression of the linear potential sweep voltammogram in the case of diffusionless electrochemical systems

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: E. Laviron, General expression of the linear potential sweep voltammogram in the case of diffusionless electrochemical systems, Journal of Electroanalytical Chemistry and Interfacial Electrochemistry, 101 (1979) 19–28.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Is the poly (methylene blue)-modified glassy carbon electrode an adequate electrode for the simple detection of thiols and amino acid-based molecules?

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M.I.C. Marinho, M.F. Cabral, L.H. Mazo, Is the poly (methylene blue)-modified glassy carbon electrode an adequate electrode for the simple detection of thiols and amino acid-based molecules?, Journal of Electroanalytical Chemistry, 685 (2012) 8–14.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Recommendations for the definition, estimation and use of the detection limit

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: C. Analytical Methods, Recommendations for the definition, estimation and use of the detection limit, Analyst, 112 (1987) 199–204.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Voltammetric Determination of Bisphenol A in Water and Juice Using a Lanthanum (III)-Doped Cobalt (II,III) Nanocube Modified Carbon Screen-Printed Electrode

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: H. Beitollahi, H. Mahmoudi Moghaddam, S. Tajik, Voltammetric Determination of Bisphenol A in Water and Juice Using a Lanthanum (III)-Doped Cobalt (II,III) Nanocube Modified Carbon Screen-Printed Electrode, Analytical Letters, 52 (2019) 1432–1444.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: CuS–MWCNT based electrochemical sensor for sensitive detection of bisphenol A

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X.C. Lu, L. Song, T.T. Ding, Y.L. Lin, C.X. Xu, CuS–MWCNT based electrochemical sensor for sensitive detection of bisphenol A, Russian Journal of Electrochemistry, 53 (2017) 366–373.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Electrochemical bisphenol A sensor based on N-doped graphene sheets

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: H. Fan, Y. Li, D. Wu, H. Ma, K. Mao, D. Fan, B. Du, H. Li, Q. Wei, Electrochemical bisphenol A sensor based on N-doped graphene sheets, Analytica chimica acta, 711 (2012) 24–28.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Electrochemical Determination of Bisphenol A Based on Poly(Chromotropic Acid) Modified Glassy Carbon Electrode

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: H. Filik, A.A. Avan, Electrochemical Determination of Bisphenol A Based on Poly(Chromotropic Acid) Modified Glassy Carbon Electrode, Current Analytical Chemistry, 13 (2017) 464–473.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Electrochemical determination of bisphenol A in plastic bottled drinking water and canned beverages using a molecularly imprinted chitosan–graphene composite film modified electrode

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: P. Deng, Z. Xu, Y. Kuang, Electrochemical determination of bisphenol A in plastic bottled drinking water and canned beverages using a molecularly imprinted chitosan–graphene composite film modified electrode, Food Chemistry, 157 (2014) 490–497.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Anderson Martin Santos received his BSc degree in Industrial Chemistry from the State University of Mato Grosso do Sul, MS (Brazil) in 2013, MS degree and PhD degree in Analytical Chemistry from the Federal University of São Carlos, SP (Brazil). Currently, he is making post-doctoral from the Federal University of São Carlos, SP (Brazil). His research interests include the development of novel electroanalytical methods for analyzes of interests diverse using different nanoarchitectures as carbon materials, metal and metal oxides nanoparticles for confection of electrochemical sensors.

Maria Helena Alves Feitosa has a degree in Chemistry from the Federal Institute of Education, Science and Technology of Piauí in Parnaíba (Brazil). Completed her MSc in Analytical Chemistry from the Federal University of São Carlos, SP (Brazil) in 2020. Currently, she is pursuing a PhD in Analytical Chemistry from the Federal University of São Carlos, SP (Brazil). Works with a focus on the construction of photoanodes from semiconductor materials applied to the degradation of drugs in environmental matrices, as well as studies on the detection of emerging contaminants manufactured with carbonaceous materials.

Ademar Wong received his PhD degree in Analytical Chemistry from São Paulo State University, Araraquara, SP (Brazil). Currently, he is making post-doctoral from the Institute of Chemistry of UNESP in Araraquara, São Paulo (Brazil). His research interests include the study of new electrode materials, metallic complex (porphyrin and phthalocyanine), molecularly imprinted polymers, nanostructured materials (carbon nanotube, carbon black and graphene oxide) and metallic nanoparticles (gold, silver, nickel oxide and copper).

Orlando Fatibello-Filho is a full Professor in Analytical Chemistry at the Federal University of São Carlos, Department of Chemistry and, head of the LABBES (http://www.labbes.ufscar.br). He received his PhD degree in Analytical Chemistry from the University of São Paulo, São Paulo, SP (Brazil) in 1985 and held a postdoctoral position from 1987 to 1989 at the University of New Orleans (USA), Department of Chemistry. He was a visiting professor at University of Coimbra in the Christopher and Ana Maria Brett Group. His research interests include the development of analytical procedures employing chemical and electrochemical sensors, biosensors, solid-phase reactors, and flow-injection systems and their applications in the determination of analytes in pharmaceutical formulations as well as environmental and food samples.

Maria del Pilar Taboada Sotomayor studied Chemistry at the Universidad Nacional de Ingeniería (Lima, Peru) from 1987 to 1992, where she obtained her BSc degree in Chemistry. In 1996 she obtained her MSc degree in Analytical Chemistry at UNICAMP (Brazil), and in March 2000 received her PhD in Sciences at UNICAMP. She is currently assistant professor and coordinator of the undergraduate course at the Institute of Chemistry of UNESP in Araraquara, São Paulo (Brazil). She works mainly in the development of electrochemical and optical biomimetic sensors based on bioinspired catalysts and molecularly imprinted polymer.

Fernando Cruz de Moraes received his B.Sc. degree in Chemistry from Federal University of São Carlos, in 2002. He received his M.S. degree in 2006 from Chemistry Institute of São Carlos – University of São Paulo and Ph.D.in 2010 in Physical Chemistry from Federal University of São Carlos, in Brazil. He has been contracted in 2016 as assistant professor at Chemistry Department of the Federal University of São Carlos. His research areas of interest include electroanalysis, biosensors, environmental and biomedical analysis, synthesis and characterization of carbon nano in photo-electrochemical devices. He is a member of the International Society of Electrochemistry (ISE), Sociedad Iberoamericana de Electroquímica (SIBAE) and Sociedade Brasileira de Eletroquímica e Eletroanalítica (SBEE).
